nodes.summary: Returns a Vite plugin that, on each dev-server page load, fetches the running Hermes dashboard's index.html to scrape and re-inject the session token and embedded-chat flag into the dev HTML, preventing 401 errors on protected /api/* routes.

layers.name: Entry Points
layers.description: Top-level Python launchers, shell wrappers, MCP server entrypoint, and shared root-level helpers that bootstrap the hermes-agent runtime (CLI, RL CLI, batch runner, MCP serve).

layers.name: Agent Core
layers.description: The core self-improving agent loop, context engine, curator, auxiliary client, and provider adapters (Anthropic, Bedrock, Codex, Gemini, Copilot) plus cron-scheduled agent jobs.

layers.name: CLI Surface
layers.description: User-facing CLI commands, authentication flows, configuration management, web server bootstrap, and interactive setup wizards delivered through hermes_cli.

layers.name: Messaging Gateway
layers.description: Multi-platform messaging gateway with adapters for Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/Teams plus the TUI gateway bridge and the Anthropic Computer Protocol (ACP) adapter.

layers.name: Tools
layers.description: The 80+ tool implementations the agent calls at runtime — browser, file, shell, voice, MCP, memory, and other capability modules under tools/ (excluding execution environments).

layers.name: Execution Environments
layers.description: Sandboxed execution backends (Local, Docker, Modal, Daytona, SSH, Vercel, Singularity) under tools/environments together with the top-level environments/ RL and agentic-evaluation environments.

layers.name: Skills
layers.description: The 425+ built-in skill packs — declarative SKILL.md descriptors, schemas, helper scripts and assets that the agent loads at runtime to acquire domain capabilities.

layers.name: Plugins & Provider Profiles
layers.description: Pluggable extensions (model providers, image generators, memory backends, platform integrations) along with the base provider-profile abstraction in providers/ that they implement.

layers.name: Frontends
layers.description: User-facing client applications: the TypeScript/React browser dashboard under web/ and the TypeScript/Ink terminal UI under ui-tui/.

layers.name: Build, Infrastructure & Documentation
layers.description: Containerization (Dockerfile, docker-compose, docker/), CI/CD workflows under .github/, install/utility scripts, project configs (pyproject.toml, package.json), and root-level documentation (README, AGENTS, CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY).

tour.title: Project Overview & Contributor Guide
tour.description: Start with the README and AGENTS guide to understand what hermes-agent is: a self-improving AI agent from Nous Research that runs in your terminal, hooks into messaging platforms (Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/Email), invokes 80+ tools across sandboxed execution backends, and grows its own skill library through a learning loop. The README is the user-facing quickstart, while AGENTS.md is the in-repo contributor guide that codifies the codebase's structure and conventions for anyone (human or AI) modifying the project.

tour.title: Entry Points: The Three Front Doors
tour.description: Hermes has three coordinated entry points. The hermes shell shim resolves the project root and forwards to Python; cli.py launches the interactive TUI REPL (HermesCLI with prompt-toolkit, slash commands, and attachment handling); and gateway/run.py is the long-running messaging gateway that hosts platform adapters and dispatches agent sessions. Both Python entry points share the same agent core in run_agent.py, so the same brain runs whether you chat in the terminal or via Telegram.

tour.title: Shared Foundations: Constants, Config, Utils
tour.description: Almost every file in the codebase imports from these three modules — they have the highest fan-in by a wide margin. hermes_constants.py resolves where the hermes home/config/skills directories live and detects platform quirks (Termux, WSL, container). hermes_cli/config.py is the config core that loads, validates, and migrates user/project configuration (providers, custom models, skills). utils.py provides atomic JSON/YAML writes, env-var coercion, and proxy URL helpers. Understand these first; you will see them everywhere.
tour.languageLesson: Python's atomic file writes (write to temp + os.replace) prevent partial writes on crash. Hermes uses this pattern in utils.py because config files are mutated frequently from multiple processes (CLI, gateway, cron).

tour.title: The Agent Core Loop
tour.description: run_agent.py defines the AIAgent class — the heart of hermes. It orchestrates the conversation loop: prepare messages, call the LLM through a transport, parse tool calls, execute them, append results, and repeat. The context engine tracks token budgets and decides when to compress history. The auxiliary client is a separate LLM dispatcher used for background tasks (titles, summaries, curation) with its own credential pool and failover. Together these three files implement the inner loop everything else feeds into.

tour.title: Provider Adapter Pattern
tour.description: Hermes supports any LLM provider via two complementary abstractions. ProviderProfile (providers/base.py) is the declarative dataclass capturing endpoints, auth, headers, and model lists, with override hooks for message preparation. The transports/ subsystem implements wire-level protocols (Anthropic, Bedrock, chat-completions, Codex), all behind the abstract ProviderTransport base. providers/__init__.py is the discovery layer that loads bundled and user-installed profiles via importlib. This split — declarative profile + protocol transport — lets new providers ship as a tiny plugin without touching the agent loop.
tour.languageLesson: Python dataclasses + importlib-based plugin discovery is a common pattern for extensible CLIs. The ProviderProfile dataclass declares config; importlib walks plugin directories and registers each profile, so users can add a provider with just a Python file and a plugin.yaml — no code changes to the core.

tour.title: The Tool System
tour.description: tools/registry.py is the central catalog: it discovers tool modules, registers them with schema metadata, caches requirement checks, and provides standard result/error helpers used everywhere. tools/__init__.py re-exports the headline tools (browser, terminal, web) as the package's public surface. terminal_tool.py shows how a single tool routes to multiple backends — local, Docker, Modal, Daytona, SSH, Singularity, Vercel — while browser_tool.py demonstrates the same pattern with Chromium, Lightpanda, CDP, and cloud providers. This decoupling between tool surface and backend is the key design pattern of the tools layer.

tour.title: Execution Environments (Sandboxed Backends)
tour.description: The terminal_tool from Step 6 dispatches to a BaseEnvironment implementation, one per sandbox. LocalEnvironment runs commands on the host with safe-cwd resolution and env sanitization. DockerEnvironment builds security args and resolves host-user specs. ModalEnvironment runs inside Modal's serverless containers with snapshotting. Daytona/SSH/Vercel provide cloud-workspace variants for remote execution. Each environment shares the same interface so any tool can pick its sandbox at runtime — the foundation of hermes's portability story.

tour.title: The Skills System (Learning Loop)
tour.description: Skills are the project's distinctive feature — declarative SKILL.md packs the agent invokes as slash-commands. agent/skill_commands.py discovers, preprocesses, and dispatches user-installed skill commands, building prompt blocks for invocation. agent/curator.py closes the learning loop: it periodically reviews skills via an LLM, classifies them as absorbed/renamed/removed, generates run reports, and applies transitions automatically. skills_hub.py is the CLI surface for searching, installing, auditing, and publishing skills, while a representative SKILL.md shows the markdown-front-matter format used by all 425+ built-in packs.
tour.languageLesson: SKILL.md packs are markdown with YAML front-matter describing inputs, outputs, and invocation hints. This 'documentation is code' pattern lets non-programmers author capabilities — the markdown body is the prompt the agent reads when the skill fires.

tour.title: Messaging Gateway & Platform Adapters
tour.description: gateway/run.py (the highest-fan-out file in the project at 102 outgoing edges) orchestrates platform adapters, session lifecycle, agent dispatch, cron ticking, and process management. gateway/config.py defines the GatewayConfig dataclass hierarchy with env-var overrides and per-platform settings. BasePlatformAdapter (platforms/base.py) defines the contract every adapter implements: MessageEvent, SendResult, EphemeralReply, and shared utilities for proxying, media caching, and channel resolution. Each concrete adapter — Telegram, Discord, Slack — adheres to this interface so the gateway can dispatch any inbound message through the same agent pipeline.

tour.title: More Platform Adapters: WhatsApp, Signal, Email
tour.description: The remaining platform adapters extend the gateway pattern to harder cases. WhatsApp supervises a Node-based bridge subprocess (since Meta has no public bot API). Signal integrates with signal-cli/signald for end-to-end-encrypted messaging. The email adapter treats threads as agent sessions, polling IMAP for inbound MIME, decoding attachments, and replying via SMTP. These three demonstrate how the BasePlatformAdapter contract from Step 9 absorbs vastly different transport realities (subprocess bridges, encrypted CLIs, mail polling) behind one uniform agent-dispatch surface.

tour.title: Plugins: Model Providers, Image Gen, Memory
tour.description: The plugins/ tree is where third-party extensions live. Model-provider plugins are tiny — the Anthropic plugin is a ProviderProfile (from Step 5) tailored for Anthropic's API; the Vercel AI Gateway plugin just injects routing kwargs. Image-gen plugins wrap providers' image APIs into the tools layer. Memory plugins (e.g. ByteRover) wrap external CLIs to persist agent memories. Each plugin pairs a small Python module with a plugin.yaml manifest that hermes_cli/plugins.py loads at startup, exposing manifests, contexts, and toolsets to the rest of the CLI.

tour.title: CLI Surface: Setup, Auth, Doctor
tour.description: hermes_cli/main.py wires up all argparse subcommands and dispatches to the submodules. The setup wizard walks new users through provider, gateway, tools, TTS, and terminal-backend choices. auth.py is the massive credentials core — OAuth/device-code flows for Anthropic, OpenAI, Codex, Nous, Bedrock, and Google plus interactive provider/model pickers. doctor.py probes the running install for misconfigurations. tools_config.py is the interactive tools wizard for MCP, image-gen, and per-platform toolset toggles. Together these commands are how users go from `pip install` to a working agent.

tour.title: Frontends: Web Dashboard & TUI Gateway
tour.description: Hermes ships two frontends. The TypeScript/React dashboard (web/) is a SPA: main.tsx bootstraps i18n, API, plugin, and theme providers; App.tsx assembles plugin-aware routing and the responsive shell. It's served by hermes_cli/web_server.py — a FastAPI app exposing status, sessions, OAuth flows, plugin management, and websockets. The TUI gateway is the other path: server.py runs a JSON-RPC dispatch loop over stdio/WebSocket, with render.py providing message/diff formatters and entry.py acting as the sidecar process bootstrap. Same agent core, two very different UX surfaces.
tour.languageLesson: React provider composition (i18n → API → plugins → theme around the app root) is how SPAs assemble cross-cutting concerns without prop drilling. The order matters: plugins typically need API context, themes consume i18n direction (LTR/RTL), so outer providers must resolve first.

tour.title: Cron: Scheduled Agent Runs
tour.description: Hermes can run itself on a schedule, not just react to messages. cron/jobs.py is the job store: it parses schedule expressions, persists jobs to disk with secure permissions, and computes next-run times. cron/scheduler.py is the tick loop — it builds prompts, invokes the agent runtime from Step 4, scans output for prompt injection, and routes results to delivery platforms (gateway adapters from Steps 9-10, home targets, plugin env-driven channels). hermes_cli/cron.py exposes the user-facing `hermes cron` commands for listing, creating, and inspecting jobs. This is how scheduled summaries, monitors, and recurring research run autonomously.

tour.title: Build, Containerization & CI/CD
tour.description: The deployment story closes the loop. The Dockerfile builds the hermes-agent runtime with Python dependencies and tool prerequisites. docker-compose.yml orchestrates the container plus supporting services for local dev and gateway deployments. docker/entrypoint.sh is the runtime glue — it drops root via gosu, fixes volume ownership, bootstraps config files (.env, config.yaml, SOUL.md, auth.json) into the mounted data volume, optionally launches the dashboard from Step 13, then execs the CLI. The docker-publish GitHub Actions workflow builds, scans, and publishes multi-arch images on releases and main pushes. This is how a `pip install` codebase becomes a production-ready container.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds and a gosu-based entrypoint solve two real problems: image size (separate build vs runtime layers) and the 'root-in-container' security issue (start as root only long enough to chown mounted volumes, then drop to a non-privileged user with exec). Layer ordering matters too — put rarely-changing system packages before frequently-changing app code so Docker's layer cache stays hot.
